text.title: Solo Incident Triage Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules the solo operator must enforce in the first 15 minutes of a production incident, in this order.

rule.statement: The first action is to stop the bleed, not to find the cause. Acceptable stop-bleed actions: roll back the last deploy, toggle a feature flag off, scale a stuck worker to zero, route traffic to a maintenance page. Cause-hunting before stop-bleed is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Mean-time-to-recovery dominates mean-time-to-cause for customer impact. A 2-minute rollback that restores service beats a 40-minute root-cause that ships a perfect fix.

rule.statement: Before applying any fix or rollback, capture: (a) timestamp of the alert, (b) error rate / latency at alert time, (c) last 3 deploy SHAs with timestamps, (d) current value of any feature flag touched in the last 24h. Write these into the incident log file in one block.
rule.rationale: Snapshots are lost as soon as you start fixing. Without them the post-incident review degenerates into reconstructed memory.

rule.statement: Set a wall-clock 15-minute deadline at the moment of acknowledging the alert. If the bleed is not stopped by the deadline, the operator MUST switch to the documented rollback runbook or post a status-page incident, even if a fix feels "close".
rule.rationale: Solo operators chronically under-estimate fix time. A hard deadline converts hope into a decision point.

rule.statement: All incident activity goes into one append-only file (`~/incidents/YYYY-MM-DD-slug.md`). One line per action with timestamp. No parallel Slack DMs, no scratch notes, no memory.
rule.rationale: A solo responder cannot reconstruct the timeline from Slack history a week later. Single-file logs become the post-mortem artefact for free.

rule.statement: No code change, no DB migration, no config edit is permitted during triage unless backed by a log line, metric delta, or error trace cited in the incident log. "I think it's X" is rejected as evidence.
rule.rationale: Stress-driven guesses during incidents cause more outages than they fix. Forcing evidence citation slows panic and surfaces the actual fault.

example.code: ## 2026-05-20 14:03 db-pool-exhausted - 14:03 alert: api 5xx rate 14% - 14:04 snapshot: rate=14%, p95=8s, deploys: a1b2c3 (13:55), prev=9f8e7d (yesterday) - 14:05 stop-bleed: rollback to 9f8e7d via deploy-gh.sh - 14:08 verified: 5xx rate back to 0.2% - 14:09 evidence: db pool log "FATAL: too many connections" at 13:58 - 14:30 root cause: a1b2c3 opened a connection in module import; fix on branch
example.note: Stop-bleed at minute 5, deadline never hit, evidence cited before re-deploy.

antipattern.description: Operator opens DataDog, scrolls logs for 25 minutes hunting for the cause while users keep hitting 500s.
antipattern.reason: Violates r1 (bleed not stopped) and r3 (deadline ignored). Customer impact compounds linearly with time-to-stop-bleed.
